bytes.topic: Architecture & Structure — feature-based folder structure, colocation, barrel exports
bytes.title: SolidJS Feature-Based Structure — Colocation Without the Barrel Export Tax
bytes.body:
SolidJS's fine-grained reactivity model makes colocation especially powerful: signals, stores, and derived computations belong next to the components that own them. Unlike React, there's no reconciler overhead from re-exporting primitives through barrel files — but barrel exports still hurt tree-shaking in Vite/Rollup builds if you're not careful.

Organize by feature, not by type. A `features/editor/` folder should own `EditorStore.ts` (a `createStore` slice), `EditorToolbar.tsx`, `useEditorShortcuts.ts` (a custom primitive wrapping `createEffect`), and its own `index.ts` that exports only the public surface.

Avoid deep barrel chains. A root `src/index.ts` that re-exports every feature barrel forces the bundler to evaluate all modules eagerly. Instead, import directly from feature paths in routing: `import { EditorPage } from '~/features/editor'`. SolidJS's `lazy()` wraps these naturally — `const EditorPage = lazy(() => import('~/features/editor'))` — giving you code-split boundaries that align with your folder boundaries. Keep signals and stores private unless explicitly exported; this enforces encapsulation at the module level.

bytes.topic: Component Design — single responsibility, 150-line rule, presentation vs logic separation
bytes.title: SolidJS Component Design — Single Responsibility and the Primitive/View Split
bytes.body:
SolidJS components run once — signals and memos handle updates, not re-renders. This makes the presentation/logic split even more natural: your JSX is static structure, your reactive primitives are the live layer.

Apply the 150-line rule by extracting logic into custom primitives. A primitive is just a function that returns signals, memos, or resources — no special hooks API needed. This keeps components thin and makes reactive logic independently testable.

The pattern: write a `createXxx` function that owns all reactive state, then pass its return value into a presentation component containing only JSX. The presentation component receives accessors (functions) as props — never destructure them directly or you sever reactivity. Use `splitProps` if forwarding a subset to avoid the same trap.

`createMemo` belongs in the logic layer, not inline in JSX. Derived values computed inline re-evaluate on every DOM expression pass; a memo caches and short-circuits updates. One responsibility per primitive: `createUserSearch` owns query state, `createUserPagination` owns page state — compose both at the container level, keeping each primitive and each view under 150 lines.

bytes.topic: State Management — local vs lifted vs context vs external store, when to use each
bytes.title: SolidJS State Management — Local, Lifted, Context, and Stores Explained
bytes.body: SolidJS gives you four distinct state primitives, each with a clear use case. Use `createSignal` for local, component-scoped state — it's reactive and self-contained. When a parent needs to coordinate two siblings, lift the signal up and pass the setter as a prop; SolidJS props are reactive, so children re-render only when the value changes. For cross-tree sharing without prop-drilling, wrap a signal in `createContext` and expose it via a provider — consumers call `useContext` and receive live reactive access. For complex, structured state (nested objects, arrays, derived slices), reach for `createStore`. It gives you fine-grained, path-level reactivity via `produce`-style mutations or direct path setters, so only the exact DOM node bound to `state.items[2].name` updates. The key SolidJS distinction: stores use Proxy-based tracking, meaning reads inside JSX automatically subscribe to only the accessed paths — no selectors needed. External libraries like `@solid-primitives/signal-store` extend this further for shared global state across module boundaries.

bytes.topic: Performance — memoization (useMemo/useCallback/computed), when it helps vs hurts
bytes.title: SolidJS createMemo — When Fine-Grained Reactivity Makes Memoization a Non-Issue
bytes.body:
SolidJS components run exactly once. There are no re-renders, so React-style `useMemo`/`useCallback` anxiety simply doesn't apply. The relevant primitive is `createMemo()`, which creates a derived signal that recomputes only when its reactive dependencies change — automatically tracked, no dependency array required.

Use `createMemo()` when a derived value is expensive to compute (e.g., filtering/sorting large arrays from signals) or when you want to prevent downstream effects and components from re-running on every upstream change. Without it, every subscriber reruns even if the derived value is identical.

Where it hurts: wrapping trivial computations (`() => count() * 2`) adds memo overhead — internal subscription tracking, lazy evaluation, and equality checks — with no measurable benefit. Prefer inline reads for cheap expressions.

Also avoid nesting `createMemo()` calls deeply when a single reactive expression suffices. SolidJS's fine-grained graph already minimizes unnecessary work; over-memoizing adds nodes to the dependency graph without reducing recomputation. The rule: memo when downstream cost exceeds memo overhead, not by default.

bytes.topic: Data Fetching — server state vs client state, TanStack Query patterns
bytes.title: SolidJS Data Fetching — Server State vs Client State with TanStack Query
bytes.body:
SolidJS makes the client/server state split explicit through its primitives. Client state — filters, toggles, form inputs — belongs in `createSignal`. Server state — remote data with caching, staleness, and revalidation — belongs in `createResource` or `@tanstack/solid-query`.

`createResource` is the built-in primitive: it integrates natively with `<Suspense>` and `<ErrorBoundary>`. For advanced needs (background refetch, optimistic updates, deduplication), reach for `@tanstack/solid-query`, which exports `createQuery` and `createMutation` — note the `create` prefix, not React's `use`.

The critical SolidJS-specific detail: `createQuery` accepts a reactive accessor `() => options`, not a plain object. This means SolidJS's tracking context picks up any signals accessed inside it. Changing `filter()` automatically triggers a new fetch — no dependency arrays, no `useEffect`.

Unlike React Query, you never call `createQuery` conditionally. The reactive accessor pattern handles branching. For mutations, `createMutation` returns a `mutate` function; wire `onSuccess` to `queryClient.invalidateQueries` to sync caches. Never use `createSignal` to store fetched API responses — that bypasses staleness tracking entirely.

bytes.topic: Forms — controlled vs uncontrolled, React Hook Form + Zod pattern
bytes.title: SolidJS Forms — Controlled Signals, Uncontrolled Refs, and Modular Forms + Zod
bytes.body:
SolidJS controlled forms bind a signal directly to an input: `value={name()}` + `onInput={e => setName(e.target.value)}`. Unlike React, this doesn't re-render a component — SolidJS surgically updates only that DOM node. Uncontrolled forms skip signals entirely: declare `let ref!: HTMLInputElement`, attach with `<input ref={ref} />`, and read `ref.value` on submit. Prefer uncontrolled when you only need values at submission time and want zero reactive overhead.

For complex forms with validation, reach for `@modular-forms/solid` — the SolidJS-native answer to React Hook Form. It uses stores (not signals) internally, so each field update is granular. Pair it with a Zod schema via `zodForm()` adapter for type-safe validation. Field components use `<Field>` with a render prop exposing `field` (value, error) and `props` (spread onto your input). The `handleSubmit` wrapper calls your action only after Zod passes — no manual `.safeParse()` needed. Crucially, the form store is reactive: `form.invalid` and `form.dirty` are live, so you can gate your submit button with `<Show when={!form.invalid}>` without any extra state.

bytes.topic: TypeScript — prop typing, discriminated unions for variants, no any
bytes.title: SolidJS Prop Typing — Discriminated Unions for Bulletproof Component Variants
bytes.body:
SolidJS components receive props as a single reactive object, so TypeScript types must account for Solid's `JSX.Element` return and its accessor-based reactivity. For variant components — buttons, alerts, badges — discriminated unions eliminate runtime guesswork and enforce exhaustive handling at compile time.

Define a union on a `kind` literal, then use a type-narrowed `switch` inside a `<Show>` or direct JSX conditional. Avoid `any` on props entirely; if an external value is untyped, cast it at the boundary with a validated type guard, not inline `as any`.

Crucial Solid-specific detail: destructuring props breaks reactivity. Use `props.kind`, never `const { kind } = props`. If you need local aliases, use `splitProps` from `solid-js`, which preserves the reactive proxy. This keeps variant logic reactive — if a parent signal changes `kind`, the component re-evaluates correctly without a full re-render cycle, because Solid tracks fine-grained signal reads, not component-level diffing.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — error boundaries per route, handle loading/error/empty states
bytes.title: SolidJS Error Boundaries — Per-Route Isolation for Loading, Error, and Empty States
bytes.body:
SolidJS's reactive primitives make state handling composable — but error boundaries require deliberate placement per route. Wrapping each route component with `ErrorBoundary` from `solid-js` isolates failures so one broken route can't crash the shell. Unlike React, SolidJS's `ErrorBoundary` passes both the error and a `reset` callback as fallback arguments, letting you trigger re-renders reactively without external state.

For loading states, `Suspense` integrates directly with `createResource` — suspension is automatic when a resource is pending. Nesting `Suspense` *inside* `ErrorBoundary` (not the reverse) is critical: thrown promises must be caught by `Suspense`, not `ErrorBoundary`. Reversing the order causes pending resources to be swallowed as errors.

Empty states belong in a `Show` component beneath `Suspense`. Since `createResource` returns `undefined` while loading and the resolved value after, `Show`'s `when` prop cleanly handles both null and empty data without additional signals. Collocate all three — `ErrorBoundary` → `Suspense` → `Show` — at the route level so each route owns its full async lifecycle.

bytes.topic: Testing — test behaviour not implementation, Testing Library philosophy
bytes.title: SolidJS Testing — Test Behaviour, Not Signals
bytes.body:
The Testing Library philosophy — query by role, test what users see — applies to SolidJS with a critical caveat: SolidJS's fine-grained reactivity runs synchronously, but DOM updates from signals still require `waitFor` or wrapping in `act` equivalents when triggered asynchronously. Use `@solidjs/testing-library` and never assert on signal values directly. Assert on the DOM.

Avoid testing that `setCount` was called or that a signal holds a specific value. Instead, fire an event and assert the rendered output changed. This decouples tests from implementation — you can refactor from `createSignal` to a `createStore` slice and your tests stay green.

`render` from `@solidjs/testing-library` mounts inside a real DOM (via jsdom/happy-dom), so `createEffect` and `createMemo` run as they would in production. Use `screen.getByRole`, fire with `userEvent`, and assert with `toHaveTextContent`. Never reach into the component closure to read signal values — if the DOM doesn't show it, the user doesn't see it.

bytes.topic: Security — dangerouslySetInnerHTML, token storage, input sanitization
bytes.title: SolidJS Security — innerHTML, Token Storage, and Input Sanitization
bytes.body: Unlike React's `dangerouslySetInnerHTML`, SolidJS accepts `innerHTML` directly as a prop with no ceremony — no warning, no friction. That silence is the danger. `<div innerHTML={userContent} />` executes any script tags or event handlers embedded in `userContent` verbatim. Always sanitize before binding. DOMPurify integrates cleanly: wrap raw HTML in `createMemo` so sanitization reruns reactively whenever the signal updates, not on every render tick. For token storage, avoid `localStorage` — it's readable by any injected script. Prefer `httpOnly` cookies managed server-side. If you must store client-side state (e.g. CSRF tokens), use a SolidJS `createStore` scoped to a short session and clear it on `onCleanup`. For form inputs, never pass `event.target.value` directly into a `createSignal` that feeds an `innerHTML` binding. Treat the signal as a trust boundary: sanitize at write time, not read time, so downstream derivations (createMemo, createEffect) always consume clean data.

bytes.topic: Code Quality — ESLint react-hooks plugin, naming conventions, 50-line rule
bytes.title: SolidJS Code Quality — Why eslint-plugin-react-hooks Will Lie to You
bytes.body:
SolidJS's reactivity model has nothing in common with React's Rules of Hooks — yet teams migrating from React often leave eslint-plugin-react-hooks installed. This creates constant noise: the plugin flags createSignal calls inside conditionals as violations, even though SolidJS primitives have no top-level placement requirement.

Replace it with eslint-plugin-solid. Two rules matter immediately: solid/reactivity catches reactive tracking loss (e.g., reading a signal outside a tracking scope), and solid/no-destructure prevents const { x } = props — the most common SolidJS bug for React developers, since it breaks the reactive proxy entirely.

For naming conventions: signal accessors are nouns (count, not getCount) — the call syntax count() already signals invocation. Setters follow setX. Derived computations belong in createMemo, not inline JSX expressions; name memos for what they represent (filteredItems, not getFilteredItems).

Apply the 50-line rule at the component boundary. When a SolidJS component grows past 50 lines, extract either a sub-component or a create* primitive factory — createPagination(), createIntersectionObserver(). This keeps components declarative and makes primitives independently testable without a component render tree.

bytes.topic: Accessibility — semantic HTML first, focus management, ARIA only when HTML falls short
bytes.title: SolidJS Accessibility — Semantic HTML First, ARIA Only When You Must
bytes.body:
SolidJS's fine-grained reactivity is a hidden accessibility ally — elements update in-place rather than unmounting, so keyboard focus is rarely displaced mid-interaction. Unlike React's reconciliation, a toggled signal won't unmount your focused button. Lean on this.

Start with semantic HTML: `<button>` not `<div onClick>`, `<dialog>` not `<div role="dialog">`. Native elements carry built-in keyboard behavior and implicit ARIA roles for free. Only reach for `aria-*` when no native element fits — custom comboboxes, live regions, or composite widgets.

For programmatic focus, attach a `ref` and drive it from `createEffect`. Effects run after DOM commits, so `ref.focus()` reliably targets the correct node. Pair with `<Show>` — its `fallback` is fully removed from the DOM, so focus cannot land on hidden content.

Avoid `aria-hidden` hacks to mask misused `<div>` buttons. Fix the element first. When ARIA is genuinely needed — like `aria-live` for async notifications — wire it to a signal so screen readers receive the update precisely when the DOM changes, not on a polling delay.

bytes.topic: Internationalization — never hardcode strings, react-i18next, Intl API for dates/numbers
bytes.title: SolidJS Internationalization — Signals, @solid-primitives/i18n, and the Intl API
bytes.body:
Hardcoding strings in SolidJS components kills localization before it starts. Use `@solid-primitives/i18n` — it's built around signals, so locale switches trigger fine-grained reactive updates without re-rendering entire subtrees.

Define a `createI18nContext` with a dictionary object, then consume translations via the returned `t()` accessor inside your JSX. Because `t()` is reactive, only the text nodes that actually changed will update — no virtual DOM diffing needed.

For dates and numbers, skip moment.js entirely. The native `Intl.DateTimeFormat` and `Intl.NumberFormat` APIs are fast and tree-shake friendly. Wrap them in a `createMemo` keyed to your locale signal so formatting recomputes only when the locale changes, not on every render.

Never store formatted strings in `createSignal` and manually invalidate them — let `createMemo` track the locale dependency automatically. Use `createResource` if translations are loaded asynchronously from a CDN, pairing it with `<Suspense>` to stream locale bundles without blocking the initial paint.

bytes.topic: Web Vitals — LCP, CLS, INP and how component patterns affect each
bytes.title: SolidJS Web Vitals — How Signals, Suspense, and lazy() Move Your LCP, CLS, and INP
bytes.body:
SolidJS's fine-grained reactivity changes how each Web Vital behaves versus VDOM frameworks.

**LCP**: `lazy()` is a bundle-splitter — never wrap above-the-fold components with it. LCP content must live in the critical chunk. In SolidStart, pass `{ deferStream: true }` to `createResource` to block the SSR shell until the resource resolves, embedding data in the initial HTML rather than a streamed patch that arrives later.

**CLS**: `Suspense` fallbacks must physically reserve the space the resolved content will occupy — a zero-height skeleton causes a measurable shift. During refetch, reach for `resource.latest`: it returns the last successful value while `resource.loading` is `true`, preventing the collapse-and-reflow cycle that tanks CLS scores.

**INP**: Fine-grained reactivity is your baseline advantage — SolidJS patches only the DOM nodes subscribed to the changed signal, not an entire subtree. However, calling multiple `setSignal()` functions in one event handler schedules separate synchronous flushes. Wrap them in `batch()` to coalesce writes into a single DOM commit, minimising time-to-paint for that interaction.

bytes.topic: Memory Leaks — cleanup in useEffect/onUnmounted/onDestroy, AbortController, removeEventListener
bytes.title: SolidJS Memory Leaks — Mastering onCleanup for Effects, Fetches, and Events
bytes.body:
SolidJS's `onCleanup()` is the single cleanup primitive for all reactive scopes — it runs before an effect re-executes and when a component's owner is disposed. Unlike React's `useEffect` return function or Vue's `onUnmounted`, `onCleanup` can be called anywhere inside a reactive owner, not just the root of an effect.

The most common leaks come from three sources: dangling event listeners, unaborted fetches, and uncleared timers. SolidJS's fine-grained reactivity means a `createEffect` watching a signal re-runs on every change — without `onCleanup`, each run spawns a new `AbortController` while the previous one lingers.

Call `onCleanup` at the top of `createEffect` to register teardown before any async work begins. For imperative `addEventListener` calls inside `createEffect` or `onMount`, always pair them with `removeEventListener` in a corresponding `onCleanup`. In `onMount`, the cleanup registered via `onCleanup` fires on component disposal.

Signals and stores owned by a component are automatically disposed when the component unmounts — but external side-effects like fetch requests, WebSocket connections, and timers are yours to clean up.

bytes.topic: Real-time & WebSockets — singleton connection, reconnection logic, optimistic UI on send
bytes.title: SolidJS WebSockets — Singleton Connection, Reconnection, and Optimistic UI
bytes.body: A single WebSocket connection shared app-wide is easy in SolidJS using a module-level singleton with a store for reactive state. Create the socket once outside any component, expose a `createStore`-backed message list, and push to it from `socket.onmessage`. For reconnection, track `[socket, setSocket]` in a `createSignal` and schedule a new `new WebSocket(url)` in `onclose` via `setTimeout` with exponential backoff — wrap this in a `createEffect` that cleans up the old socket on re-run. Optimistic UI on send is clean: push a `{ id: tempId, status: 'pending', ...payload }` entry into the store immediately, then reconcile with `produce` when the server acks (matching by `tempId`). Because SolidJS stores are fine-grained, only the specific message row re-renders on status change — not the entire list. Avoid putting the socket in a store directly; keep it as a plain signal so SolidJS doesn't attempt to proxy the native object. Use `onCleanup` in any component that subscribes to drain event listeners without closing the shared connection.

bytes.topic: Environment & Config — never hardcode URLs or keys, validate env vars at startup with Zod
bytes.title: SolidJS Config — Validate Vite Env Vars at Startup with Zod
bytes.body:
SolidJS apps built with Vite expose env vars via `import.meta.env`, but nothing stops a mis-named var from silently becoming `undefined` at runtime. Validate the entire env contract once at module load time using Zod — before any component mounts or any `createResource` fires.

Define a schema in `src/env.ts` and parse `import.meta.env` eagerly. Zod's `z.string().url()` rejects malformed API base URLs immediately; `z.string().min(1)` catches empty-string substitutions that Vite happily injects. Because this module is imported before `render()` in `src/index.tsx`, a bad config throws before the SolidJS reactive graph even initialises — no partial hydration, no cryptic fetch errors mid-session.

Export the typed `env` object and import it wherever you'd otherwise reach for `import.meta.env.VITE_*` directly. Signals and resources that depend on `env.VITE_API_URL` now get a guaranteed `string`, not `string | undefined`, so TypeScript stops asking you to null-check every call site.

bytes.topic: API Layer — single HTTP client instance, interceptors for auth/retry, never raw fetch in components
bytes.title: SolidJS API Layer — One HTTP Client, Interceptors, No Raw fetch in Components
bytes.body:
Raw `fetch` calls scattered across SolidJS components create retry chaos and auth token drift. Instead, centralize all HTTP traffic through a single client module that owns interceptors, base URL, and error normalization.

Create an `apiClient.ts` that wraps `fetch` with a request pipeline: inject the Clerk JWT from `useAuth()` at the interceptor boundary, not inside components. Add a retry interceptor with exponential backoff for 429/503 responses. Expose typed helpers (`get<T>`, `post<T>`) that return `Promise<T>`, keeping components free of HTTP concerns.

In SolidJS, pair this with `createResource` — your data-fetching primitive. The resource fetcher function calls `apiClient.get(url)` directly; SolidJS handles Suspense integration, reactive re-fetching on signal changes, and error boundaries via `ErrorBoundary`. This keeps component code purely declarative: signal in, data out.

Never instantiate auth headers inside a `createResource` fetcher. The client layer owns cross-cutting concerns; the resource owns reactivity. Violating this boundary makes auth token rotation a component-level emergency.

bytes.topic: Animation — CSS for simple states, Framer Motion for complex, prefers-reduced-motion
bytes.title: SolidJS Animation — CSS for States, Framer Motion for Sequences, and Respecting prefers-reduced-motion
bytes.body:
SolidJS's fine-grained reactivity makes animation straightforward: CSS handles simple state transitions via class toggling with `classList`, while Framer Motion's `motion` primitives cover complex sequences. Because SolidJS doesn't batch renders like React, transitions fire immediately when signals update — no stale-closure surprises.

For reduced-motion, read the media query once at module scope using `window.matchMedia`, or reactively via a `createSignal` tied to the `change` event. Pass this as a prop or context to conditionally swap `animate` configs in Framer Motion, or strip transition classes entirely.

Avoid wrapping every element in a `<motion.div>` — SolidJS's DOM diffing is minimal by design, so unnecessary wrappers break that contract. Prefer animating existing DOM nodes with `ref` + the Web Animations API for surgical control, or use `@motionone/solid` (the official SolidJS binding) instead of the React-targeted `framer-motion` package.

bytes.topic: Global UI Patterns — toast system at app root, modal management, confirmation dialogs as hooks
bytes.title: SolidJS Global UI Patterns — Toasts, Modals, and Confirmation Dialogs at the App Root
bytes.body:
SolidJS lets you declare reactive state outside components — a powerful pattern for global UI. Create a module-level `createStore` for toast state, expose a plain `toast()` function, and render into a `<Portal>` at the app root. No context needed for simple cases; the store is already reactive globally.

For confirmation dialogs, use a `createSignal<{ msg: string; resolve: Resolver } | null>(null)` at module scope. A `confirm(msg)` function wraps `setSignal` in a `Promise` resolved by the dialog's OK/Cancel callbacks — giving you async confirmation consumable anywhere without prop drilling or lifting state.

SolidJS's `<Show>` with the accessor overload (`{(d) => ...}`) guarantees the inner value is non-null, avoiding manual null guards. `<Portal>` mounts children into `document.body` by default, keeping DOM hierarchy clean while your reactive graph stays fully connected.

Wire `<ConfirmDialog>` and `<ToastHost>` once inside `<App>`. Consumers call `toast("Saved!")` or `const ok = await confirm("Delete?")` as plain imports — no hooks, no dispatch, just module-level signals driving fine-grained updates.

bytes.topic: Bundle & Asset Optimization — tree-shaking, lazy-load heavy components, analyze bundle
bytes.title: SolidJS Bundle Optimization — lazy(), Tree-Shaking, and Visualizing Your Build
bytes.body:
SolidJS ships a ~7kb runtime, but third-party libraries still bloat your bundle. Use `lazy()` from `solid-js` to code-split heavy components — it wraps a dynamic `import()` and integrates directly with `<Suspense>`. Unlike React, SolidJS's compiler eliminates JSX transform overhead entirely, so your tree-shaking baseline is already leaner.

For analysis, add `rollup-plugin-visualizer` to `vite.config.ts` — it outputs an interactive treemap after `vite build`. Look for duplicated polyfills or oversized charting libs that snuck in transitively.

SolidJS primitives (`createSignal`, `createMemo`, `createResource`) are individually exported from `solid-js` and fully tree-shakeable — only what you import ships. Avoid barrel re-exports from your own `utils/index.ts` files, which can defeat Rollup's static analysis. Use `sideEffects: false` in your `package.json` if you publish a component library built on Solid. For route-based splitting, `@solidjs/router` accepts lazy components natively — pair each route's `component` prop with a `lazy()` call and let the router handle the `<Suspense>` boundary.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling & Quality Gates — Prettier, Husky + lint-staged, tsc --noEmit in CI
bytes.title: SolidJS Quality Gates: Prettier, lint-staged, and tsc --noEmit That Actually Work
bytes.body:
SolidJS projects commonly skip type-checking in CI because vite-plugin-solid owns compilation — tsc never runs. That's a silent gap. Wire up three gates to catch issues before they merge. Prettier with `--parser babel-ts` handles `.tsx` files without a SolidJS-specific plugin. Drop it into lint-staged targeting `*.{ts,tsx}` alongside `eslint-plugin-solid`, which enforces signal accessor call correctness, flags missing cleanup in `onCleanup`, and prevents React-shaped patterns (e.g., calling a signal without `()` in JSX — valid in React, broken in Solid). The critical gate is `tsc --noEmit` in CI. For it to resolve SolidJS's JSX types — `Component

`, `ParentComponent`, `JSX.Element`, `Accessor` — your `tsconfig.json` must set `"jsx": "preserve"` and `"jsxImportSource": "solid-js"`. Without `jsxImportSource`, TypeScript falls back to React's JSX namespace and type errors become invisible. This catches mistyped store mutations, broken `createResource` return shapes, and prop mismatches that vite-plugin-solid compiles through without complaint.

bytes.topic: Monitoring in Production — Sentry for errors, Web Vitals reporting, source maps
bytes.title: solidjs Production Monitoring — Sentry, Web Vitals, and Source Maps
bytes.body:
solidjs apps use `@sentry/solidjs`, which wraps `@sentry/browser` with reactive-runtime awareness. Call `Sentry.init()` in `main.tsx` before `render()` — initializing after mount misses early reactive panics. For router-aware tracing with `@solidjs/router`, pass `withSentryRouterWrapper` to the `root` prop of `<Router>`.

Capture UI errors via solidjs's `<ErrorBoundary>` — its `fallback` prop receives `(err, reset)`, so call `Sentry.captureException(err)` there directly. Reactive computations that throw inside `createEffect` or `createMemo` propagate to the nearest `<ErrorBoundary>`, meaning a single boundary at the app root catches most reactive panics without per-component wiring.

For Web Vitals, wire `web-vitals` callbacks in `main.tsx` and forward via `Sentry.captureEvent`. Use `level: "info"` to keep them separate from error counts in your Sentry dashboard.

Source maps: set `build.sourcemap: true` in `vite.config.ts` and add `@sentry/vite-plugin`. The plugin uploads maps at build time and strips them from the production bundle — stack traces stay readable in Sentry without exposing source to clients.

bytes.topic: Reactivity Models — how React, Vue, Svelte, and Solid each decide what re-runs on state change
bytes.title: SolidJS Reactivity — Why Your Component Runs Once but Your UI Keeps Updating
bytes.body:
React re-runs the entire component function on every state change. Vue wraps state in ES Proxies and tracks mutations at runtime. Svelte rewrites your source at compile time, inserting `$$invalidate` calls. SolidJS does none of these.

In Solid, components are setup functions — they execute exactly once. Reactivity is owned by signals, not components. `createSignal` returns a getter/setter pair; the getter is a tracked reactive read. When you call `setCount(5)`, Solid's runtime walks the signal's subscriber graph and surgically updates only the DOM nodes that called `count()` — no reconciler, no VDOM diffing, no stale closure risk.

`createEffect` subscribes automatically to every signal accessed during its execution and re-runs when any of them change. `createMemo` is a lazy, cached derivation — it recomputes only when its signal dependencies change, not on a render cycle (there is no render cycle). This makes deeply nested derived state cheap by default.

The mental model shift: stop thinking in renders. Think in subscriptions. A Solid app is a reactive graph where signals push updates downstream through effects and memos directly to the DOM.
